sentences.text: Well, when the day of liberation, they actually opened the doors-- opened the gates between the women and men, and we gathered together.
And, at the time, my sister's boyfriend introduced me to group of young men.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And one of them was my deceased husband, Joseph Jonas.
Of course.
We only-- we were-- we were together in the factory.
But at night, we were separate.
Men and women were separate.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE] And on the day of liberation, when they let us together, we actually-- I met those young fellows.
One of them was Joe, and others who I'm still friendly with now.
And I found out later that Joe and others were-- had already formed a resistance group that Schindler was very alert to and knew about it.
They were connected with the Czechoslovakian people outside the camp.

And I was told-- and that's not that long ago-- my husband really never spoke about it too much, because we didn't really exchange that much-- that he was really one that was bringing all the information from the Czech to the group.
And they had a lot of arms, and Schindler knew about it and was helping them with it.
There are other people now who speak about it more.
They know about it.

As a matter of fact, I just met, this Sunday, one of my friends who was very friendly with my-- with Joe-- with my deceased husband-- and he was telling me that he was a very important part of the resistance group in Brunnlitz.
He-- Victor Lewis.
And he also was very active in the group.
Well, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very impressed with him.
He was a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] special person.
He was different from the others.
Very bright, very intelligent young fellow.
I think that he was self-taught about-- he already went to gymnasium before the war.
He lived in-- in Czarny Dunajec, and he went to gymnasium in Nowy Targ for engineering.
He spoke, fluently, German.
He spoke Hebrew.
He was type of a person who liked to read a lot.
He was very bright.
Well, he was similar, yes, in a way.
You always go back to the one that you love so much.
I looked up to him.
So did my children.
He-- very fine young man, with a lot of-- to give.
And we had dated since we went back to Cracow together.
I remember going to Krakow with-- which was provided by Schindler, that we-- we had trains-- were rather comfortable, with bunks in it.
And he gave us material to-- to-- for clothes.
Schindler did provide that for us, you know?
And we-- we went to Cracow.
When was the last time you saw Schindler?
Schindler I saw almost the day of-- you know, the day when-- when-- of end of the war, I remember they had four speakers in the factory.
Did you know about it?
There were-- on each side was a speaker that Schindler installed, and he told us to listen to a speech by Churchill.
Victory speech.
And, you know, everybody was standing.
I mean, how many people understood at all?
Some of the boys-- I think my husband understood a few words, here and there.
And-- but it was such excitement, because we heard, you know, the he said 'victory speech!' And we knew something is going on.
And, all of a sudden, everybody realized that we are safe.
That we are free!
That war is over.
We really were completely unaware how many of us are left.
We didn't know that so few are left.
But there was some kind of, like, I would call it electricity in the air, you know?
And also I remember, prior to it, it was Schindler's birthday.
That was, like, a couple of months of-- before-- before the end of the war.
And somebody baked a cake.
I know they did, because we were singing Happy Birthday to him.
And he was-- made a speech, you know, with his hands open, and was telling us that we are in a safe place, that no one can hurt us here.
There is no war going on here.
But also, he told us to stay in a silence for our people.
For our dead people.

Because, prior to it, there was-- I only found out this after the war-- supposedly there was a transport that was stopped-- excuse me-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [DRINKS] they brought a transport to the front of the camp, and the SS told Schindler, if he doesn't take those people, they're going to open the door and kill them all and leave them there.
It was a transport of people that was on their way for 14 days.
And Schindler said, OK, I take 'em.
What happened, they opened the doors.
Most of them were frozen dead inside.
The some that were alive were like skeleton.
And Schindler had-- some of our boys, supposedly, helped them to carry them and bring them, sort of, health-- whatever they could help them with.
Supposedly Mrs.
Schindler brought a lot of medication to help those few that survived.
But some of them that were dead, Schindler had obtained a plot for those to be buried in the Jewish tradition.
And he asked us to stay in silence, in memory of our dead.
That is very vivid in my memory.
On the day of liberation, I found out-- my husband told me-- they took Schindler out at night to his safety.
There were eight boys.
And in his car, they drove him to the border-- American border-- to his safety.
Because the Russian who liberated us-- and the Americans came by plane-- the Russian came on horses.
But the Czechs and the-- and the Russian would probably kill him, because he was a spy for the Nazi party.
So we felt, he saved us.
We have to save him.
And it was a very difficult traveling route to safety, but they got him to the American border.
And supposedly, later, when I was in a displaced person camp, in DP camp, in Austria, I recall people were coming to us, asking for money for Schindler.
And no matter what anybody had, gave.
But you also sign our names.
That's why my husband found his brother, because my husband had two brothers.
One died with the parents.
But one, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Joe was told, was also not alive.
However, one day I was in Austria, and I see a young boy came asking for-- for my fiance's name.
Is Joseph Jonas living here?
And I said, who are you?
He said, I am his brother.
What happened is, he met Schindler, and he said, your name is Jonas?
Jonas?
He says, yes.
Do you have a brother, by Joe Jonas?
He says, yes.
See, when we were giving money, we were signing names.
So Schindler knew every name that gives money.
You see?
And when he met his brother, he told him exactly where we are, and that's how they found each other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Schindler had really not succeeded anymore afterwards.
He tried everything.
He-- he opened a cement factory in Germany.
He made a film.
Nothing really went well.
Then a Jewish agency had send him money, and he went to Argentina, where he bought a farm.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And fur-- fur-- you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] making fur.
And I don't know exactly what type of farm, but the fact is that he was there for quite a while, and he wasn't prosperous.
No, not really.
He contacted certain people that-- we struggled, from the beginning, in the United States.
It wasn't easy.
It was-- working, going to school, we just gave whatever we could.
But only people who were successful were able to, you know, to-- to be in touch with Schindler, from what I understand now.
We-- whenever we were asked for any help, we also would give, because we felt that he was our savior.
He saved us.
I think that Schindler, for whatever reason, he did what he did-- joining the Nazi party, or being a agent, or being a spy, or whatever his-- his reason was-- is his personal life.
However, when it comes to a decent human being, and when he saw what was going on, he started to act upon it.
He was there and did whatever he was able to do to save another human being.
And my reasoning for speaking out to young people is mostly pointing out the fact that we have choices in life.
I met two extremes-- Goeth and Schindler.
They were both Nazis.
They had a right to believe whatever they believe in.
But when it came to be a decent human being, they had a choice.
And Schindler made his choice, and he was a decent human being where this one was an evil man.
And this is the reason why I want young people to know that they have to be alert to people who carry hate.
Because hate brings evilness.
And we have a choice.
And no matter what people will say about Schindler-- that he was a businessman, he wanted to make money-- yes, he probably did.
But he used that money for us.
And he saved us.
He saved so many people.
I wouldn't be here.
I don't think I would be here, and my sisters definitely wouldn't be here.
By the time when I got them out from the transport, there was hardly anybody alive.
My cousins were there.
One aunt was there.
I don't-- I don't-- I don't hear from them.
So, no matter how people-- some people are angry because they were not on the list.
They were working for him in the Emalia, in Cracow, and then they were taken out, because Goeth asked Schindler to bring the people back to Plaszow.
And naturally some of them went to Mauthausen, some went somewhere else, some were working for him in the Emalia, in Cracow, and they were not on the list.
So they feel a little bit remorse, you know?
They feel, well, I was with him.
Why wasn't I on the list?
But I guess this is all-- it was meant to be-- whoever.
Fact is that he saved so many people.
And we had to do something for him, as well.
Yes, of course we do.
My sisters always tell me, thanks to you-- and my oldest sister was a little bit upset that I was not in the book.
It's about time that I bring you out.
I said, I don't care.
What happened--
Well, the movie was based on the book.
OK.
What happened with the book--
I was meaning But that was after the--
After that.
No.
What happened with the first book where, you know, Mr.
Keneally wrote-- OK-- When they started to gather people as witnesses for the book, they came to me.
They contacted me.
This was about two-- not quite two and a half months after I lost my husband.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I couldn't talk about concentration camp at the time.
It was impossible.
Now, would I-- would I-- if I would know that Helen is not around, I probably would say, somebody has to talk.
Because there were only two of us.
But I knew Helen lives in Israel.
I knew she-- whatever she will tell, that will apply the same to me.
Maybe she remembers few more incidents.
Maybe I remember a few more that apply to me.
But basically, we were like sisters, all the time.
She was older.
You know, she-- she protected me sometimes, because she spoke very well German.
But I managed, and I learned quick because I had to.
And I felt, well, you know, they only can take one character, not two or three, because otherwise the book would never end, and-- and-- and the stories would never end.
Now, later, with the movie, I was told that they will put two-- you have a scene there with two maids.
There is one scene, supposedly.
However, it's based on the book.
And my sister was a little bit upset that, somehow, I am not being spoken about.
And some of my friends say, I don't understand.
You were there.
We all saw you there.
Why aren't you around?
I said, because it doesn't matter.
The story is there.
They telling-- Helen is telling the story about what kind of a horrible monster, murderer, brutal man he was.
What he did to our people.
What he did to us.
The story was told.
Terrible.
You know, I was invited to the grand opening, to the first opening.
And the first thing Poldek said to me, I want to see your reaction, very much.
Poldek Pfefferberg is the one that gave the story-- original story.
He said, Helen, I want to see you respond.
You know, when he slapped her, when he headed downstairs, when he came down-- the scene what he's talking to his maid-- you know, I grabbed myself so, like this.
You know, I actually, physically, like, felt it!
You know?
I felt like-- like something happened to me.
Like a reaction, you know?

And naturally, you know, seeing him standing there with a-- with a gun-- with a machine gun, that horrible face, with that big stomach of his, with that miserable expression on his face, I felt I am there.
You know, it was so scary, I was holding on to myself, and I was glad I was sitting to my husband, Henry.
Because it felt like I am there.
And then, you know, the beginning, when they were marching, I just felt like I see faces of my friends in-- in this-- in this march.
And the sound of the train-- I-- I see my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't stand sound of a train.
It haunts me.
It's just always there.
You know, I really try-- try hard to enjoy what I have-- my children, my grandchildren, my friends, and everything I have now.
I really try, because I was spared.
I don't know why, but I was spared.
Why not others?
Why me?
How about your faith in God during that time?
I think he was there all the time.
Because I only knew one thing-- that God created [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] us.
And we caused-- we didn't-- people didn't help one another.
The world stood still.
Nobody did anything.
And that's why I'm speaking out now.
I-- I have difficulty speaking.
Especially today-- I don't even have my voice!
I-- I really would like to put it behind me.
I made a tape for the Heritage Museum of Jewish history, and I feel that I-- I made my peace.
I left a legacy.
It's a three and a half hour tape.
It's very thorough.
And I felt, I did it.
It's there.
It can be used.
I made a tape in the suburban temple in-- in Long Island that's being used in Hebrew schools and all over, even public schools.
I spoke there a long time ago.
So I feel that I did my duty, and it's there.
But now, Mr.
Spielberg came out with this incredible movie that it brought an awakening.
It awakened people, and people want to know.
And I was told by my friends, Helen, if you don't speak out now, who will?
You were there.
You really connect with the movie, and people believe you.
So I feel I have an obligation.
You know, I feel if I don't do it, I feel guilty.
And I feel that time is running short.
And if I can alert people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the future, if I can be of any help to-- to-- you know, to-- people recognize when there is danger.
Because we were completely unprepared.
The movie is is very, very true.
But it's-- it's just part of it.
You know?
It's-- it's just-- I personally think that, if there would be more, people wouldn't be able to deal with this.
I mean, they don't show hanging, at all.
They don't show mutilation.
They don't say-- they don't show 25 lashes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They show a small part of it.
But whatever they do show is the truth.
Therefore, people like me should come out, no matter how hard it is, and fill in the rest.
Which is almost impossible, because I don't think that, in two, three, four hours, you can really tell the story of treacherous five and a half years.
It's-- it was hard with my children, because they suffer when you tell them.
I mean, I did--
Three children.
And some of them could take more.
One of my daughters was more into it.
Shelly.
Vivian came to some of my talks.
I talked already in-- in Long Island, when I lived in Long Island.
My son had a hard time.
Now, he keeps saying-- they all keep saying to me--
Yes, my son, Steven, and my grandchildren, his grandson-- Grandma, Mom, I am so proud of you.
They all tell me, I'm so proud of you.
My grandson brings the-- the article from newspapers, my pictures, to his Hebrew school and talks about me.
And-- and I hope that those children will continue, because it should never be forgotten.
It should be carried on, because it was a horror.
The fact that we have to remember, that we have to have Israel-- we really do have to remember that.
It's essential.
This is a wonderful country.
I'm very grateful to be here.
It gave me a chance-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] another chance in life.
But if we would have Israel, the Holocaust wouldn't take place.
I'm sure of that.
No.
OK.
Well, I just had the first bar mitzvah of my grandson, Brian Jonas, and I'm always very proud how he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] conducted his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Torah.
And at the same time, something very special came out, what I am very, very proud.

He wants to give half of his money that was given to him, as gifts, for Holocaust [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] purposes, that we use-- like the museum or others-- to remember, knowing that this really happened.
He's very much into it.
And I hope that my other grandchildren will follow his steps, and they know-- know where they come from, because I know they are proud of their grandparents.
Because the rest of us are humble people.
We went through a lot.
Hitler tried to destroys-- destroy us.
But he didn't succeed.
Because we believe in God, we believe in our heritage, and most of us have wonderful children-- well-educated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we're proud of them.
We are humble people.
I speak for all of us that survived, not only for myself.
Because anytime I get together with my people and I look around, and I say, who would believe that those people were tortured to such a degree?
And now look at them.
They try so hard to be like everybody else.
And they did it the hard way-- through hard work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
You know it wasn't easy coming as a newcomer, without a language-- well, fortunately, I hired a tutor in Austria, to learn how to speak a little English with my husband, how to write and read.
But most of us had to come to a different culture, different country, different language, with nothing.
Not asking for anything from anybody, doing everything on our own.
Going to school, working.
I happened to be very fortunate that I met someone who talked me in, to go into a wonderful profession--
